expectations.expectation: Design process includes at least two investigated questions with explicit hypotheses stated before investigation
expectations.justification: No investigated-questions workflow with hypotheses is used. The document presents a direct assessment and design decisions without the hypothesis-investigation-surprise structure.

expectations.expectation: Design identifies the tension between scope (real-time + offline + version history + 500-page docs) and constraints (2 engineers, 3 months)
expectations.justification: Opens with an 'Honest Assessment: Scope vs. Constraints' section comparing requirements to Google Docs (hundreds of engineers, 15+ years). Provides a table quantifying each requirement's effort (real-time: 8-12 engineer-months, offline: 4-6, 500-page: 2-3, version history: 2-4, total: 16-25 engineer-months vs. 6 available). States: 'We must scope down.'

expectations.expectation: Design recommends scoping down or phasing the requirements rather than promising to deliver everything -- YAGNI principle is applied
expectations.justification: Two-phase approach: Phase 1 (3 months) covers real-time collaboration for ~50 pages with basic version history and sub-100ms latency. Phase 2 (months 4-6) adds offline editing, 500-page scaling, and efficient version history. Explicit 'What's Cut and Why' section explains deferrals: offline (compounding bugs on unstable collaboration), 500 pages (90% of use cases are smaller), version history (store log, build UI later).

expectations.expectation: Design chooses a specific real-time collaboration algorithm (OT or CRDT) with trade-off analysis appropriate to the constraints
expectations.justification: CRDT (Yjs) chosen over OT with a comparison table across 5 dimensions (server requirement, offline support, implementation complexity, performance, future offline support). Explains why Yjs specifically: open-source TypeScript, used by Notion/JupyterLab, provider libraries for WebSocket/IndexedDB/ProseMirror, compact encoding. 'Why Not OT' section addresses central server bottleneck, transformation function complexity, and Google's years-long stabilization.

expectations.expectation: Design recommends using an existing CRDT/OT library (e.g., Yjs, Automerge) rather than building from scratch, given the 2-engineer 3-month constraint
expectations.justification: Yjs is explicitly recommended as an existing library. States: 'Choosing Yjs dramatically reduces engineering effort from "build a CRDT from scratch" (months) to "integrate a library" (weeks).' Also recommends Hocuspocus (open-source Yjs WebSocket backend) and TipTap with y-prosemirror binding. The design is built entirely around leveraging existing libraries rather than building from scratch.

expectations.expectation: Design addresses how offline edits merge back with concurrent online edits from other collaborators (not just generic sync)
expectations.justification: Offline Support (Phase 2) section explains: edits made offline are applied to local Yjs document, WebSocket provider reconnects and 'automatically syncs -- the CRDT merge handles conflicts by design. No special conflict resolution logic needed. Two users can type in the same paragraph simultaneously (online or offline), and the CRDT guarantees convergence.' Also acknowledges semantic conflicts: 'two users rewrote the same paragraph with different intent -- the CRDT merges the characters, but the meaning might be garbled.'

expectations.expectation: Design addresses version history storage strategy that scales with 500-page documents (not naive full-document snapshots)
expectations.justification: Strategy section opens: 'Storing full document snapshots for every edit of a 500-page document would be enormously wasteful. Instead:' Describes operation log (tiny deltas per edit) + periodic snapshots (every 100 updates or every hour). Reconstruction via nearest snapshot + forward replay. Compared to database WAL + checkpoints analogy.

expectations.expectation: Design addresses the sub-100ms latency requirement with specific architectural choices (WebSocket transport, server proximity, delta-only updates)
expectations.justification: Dedicated 'Sub-100ms Latency' section with 4 strategies: WebSocket transport (no HTTP overhead), Yjs delta encoding (~30-50 bytes per keystroke), no server transformation (receive -> broadcast, sub-millisecond), server proximity (deploy in closest region). Latency budget table shows total ~20-80ms for same-region. Notes cross-region would be 100-200ms.

expectations.expectation: At least one investigated question redirects -- the investigation reveals the real decision is different from the originally framed question
expectations.justification: No investigation process with question redirection is used. The document presents direct analysis without the investigation framework that would support question redirection.

expectations.expectation: Design presentation is broken into sections with opportunity for user feedback between sections
expectations.justification: The document is presented as a continuous design spec without explicit pause points for user feedback. There are no 'speak up if you disagree' prompts or section-by-section feedback opportunities.

expectations.expectation: Gap scan is performed before saving, checking at least acceptance criteria and testing strategy
expectations.justification: No gap scan is performed. The document ends with a Summary section and a Risks section (3 items: Yjs performance, Hocuspocus stability, scope creep), but this is not a systematic gap scan checking acceptance criteria, testing strategy, failure modes, and edge cases.

eval_feedback: The without_skill version handles the core design challenge well: it identifies the scope-constraint tension, recommends aggressive scope reduction, and makes sound technical choices (Yjs, TipTap, Hocuspocus).

The new expectation about recommending existing CRDT/OT library is met: Yjs is explicitly recommended and the design is built around leveraging existing libraries (Yjs, Hocuspocus, TipTap, y-prosemirror).
The Risks section provides some value (Yjs performance, Hocuspocus stability, scope creep) but falls short of a systematic gap scan.
The 4-point gap from the with_skill version comes entirely from process expectations (hypotheses, question redirection, feedback opportunities, gap scan) rather than substance.
